title: Why the letters break
checkLabels.t1: Did you say which alphabet you use
why.no-charset: You have not told the browser which alphabet you use. It reads your numbers with the wrong dictionary — that is where the gibberish comes from. The tag goes in the <head>, at the very top, before <title>.
why.wrong-charset: There is an encoding, but it is not UTF-8. That is the alphabet that holds everything: Cyrillic, Latin, Chinese, emoji. Today there is no reason to use anything else.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
blocks.0.text: Sooner or later you will write something in a language with special letters. And then you will see this:
blocks.2.text: Gibberish. The mess every beginner sees at least once and concludes they have broken something terrible.
blocks.4.text: A computer does not store letters. It stores numbers. Every letter is a number, and the alphabet that says which number is which letter is called an encoding.
blocks.5.text: If you write using one alphabet, and the browser reads using another — it sees your numbers but translates them with the wrong dictionary. Nonsense comes out.
blocks.6.text: Tell it which alphabet you use
blocks.8.text: It goes in the <head>, right at the top — before <title>. UTF-8 is the alphabet that holds everything: Cyrillic, Latin, Chinese, emoji. Today there is no reason to use anything else.
blocks.9.text: Notice this tag has no closing twin. There is nothing to wrap — it simply announces something. There are few such tags, but they exist.
blocks.10.text: The third invisible thing in the page's head. You are starting to sense that <head> is full of things you cannot see, but without which nothing works.
